description: Dialect of the Kabenyong language spoken in the port city of Sabohai.

entries.definition: just now, in the recent past

aban o do-*é aci’* nai bebeh

examples.translation: The Hanggara are not fair to us.

examples.translation: The priest didn’t want to bless my child.

derivations.definition: low-ranking priest tasked with blessing new children, weddings, etc.

examples.translation: This food is the one for you.
The river there is very narrow.

derivations.definition: start to close up, become thin
be frustrated due to mental overload or confusion

Show me both of your hands.

This fruit doesn’t have much flesh.
Remove some water from the broth

examples.translation: Don’t expect there to be someone to clean up after you
He should go, he is allowed to go.

He’s the one who knocked over the vessel.

derivations.definition: push something off, cause to fall with great intent

derivations.definition: say odd things that don’t make sense

derivations.definition: daydream, let thoughts wander, lost in thought

examples.original: dé o doh-*oken~aken* meléh pa inang

examples.original: dé o do-*boh aken~aken* pa tin

entries.definition: time in far past; a long time ago

examples.translation: Long ago, this village was a forest.

entries.definition: ancestral, passed down from generation to generation

entries.definition: to suffer, suffocate in something, perish

derivations.definition: to die a violent death (lit. “drown in blood”)

derivations.definition: to die by being buried alive

examples.original: Kakadom o doh-*onga minyau* réng lenga’ si péa ta’ tin* *do-kiro’

examples.original: tin o do-*é kat-angara-n* pa lekit
tin o *angét* ki udoh pa péa

derivations.definition: to enable, allow, grant an ability

examples.original: cogua sinjau to’ o *angkina* ki ahi’-en umau mai

entries.definition: to have (of a person’s material possessions) (for other possessives see **laba** for inalienable possessions, **hemai** for objects contained or carried, and **udoh** for possession of animate objects and communally owned objects)

It smells like a corpse here.

examples.original: Wirap o ahét *anyung-en*-au holih ki tin
hon o lu’ cé’ *kat-anyung-en*-au, geping ru’ ahét arai gelon

derivations.definition: to pull out and remove (small hairs, weeds, etc.)

derivations.definition: to run away, (of a person) disappear

entries.definition: be an only child (of a person), alone (figuratively)

examples.translation: Oh, she only has one child?

derivations.definition: to bleed, to leak, have a leak (crass) (syn. **lérah** ‘to bleed’)

entries.definition: to pass by, meet by chance
open (of containers, or doors and pathways)

derivations.definition: to fly as an arrow, with intent

dé’é wagésén o udoh *babiak* si hon

entries.definition: to talk, speak (possibly reduplicated from **bah** ?) (syn. **sahen** ‘to say something’)

derivations.definition: topic that is talked about, things said

derivations.definition: to have a casual conversation, chit-chat

examples.original: Wirap o ambana ki *bagah-bagah* si holih ki dé

empua ki dé o do-*nyo’ bagah* in adé

because (var. **goming**). Usually only found in poetry for an extra syllable.

examples.translation: The boat made a creaking sound.

aban o do-*kua bengga* bitau péa

derivations.definition: a term referring to four major sacred rivers on the Sesenjuh peninsula, which represents the four times the mother goddess has given birth, also an exonym for the people living around the four rivers used by the Hanggara. In most contexts it is applied to a specific ethnolinguistic group which the Hanggara have the most contact with.

examples.translation: There are many types of fish.

the outside or outermost part of something

Repah kin ta’ sinjau, mai oka’ aban onggit in lap taho’ pipirobai*. ‘This plant usually grows in forests around rivers. It grows sticking on trees. We use its long leaves to wrap *pipirobai* (a cone-shaped dish).’

to blow something with the mouth

spiraling tendrils of any plant, used by children for entertainment, but also as an aphrodisiac

examples.original: pa lu’ dé o *ka-darong-en*-au, wé meni’-o

Put the fruit into the basket.
